question:
5. Find the smallest volume bounded by the coordinate planes and by a tangent plane to the ellipsoid
\[
\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}+\frac{z^{2}}{c^{2}}=1
\]
solution:
Solution. The tangent plane to the given ellipsoid at the point \( \left(x_{1}, y_{1}, z_{1}\right) \) has the equation
\[
\frac{x x_{1}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y y_{1}}{b^{2}}+\frac{z z_{1}}{c^{2}}=1 .
\]

Its intercepts on the \( x, y \), and \( z \)-axes, respectively, are
\[
\frac{a^{2}}{x_{1}}, \frac{b^{2}}{y_{1}}, \frac{c^{2}}{z_{1}}
\]

The volume of the solid cut off by the tangent plane and the three coordinate planes is
\[
V=\frac{1}{6}\left|\frac{a^{2} b^{2} c^{2}}{x_{1} y_{1} z_{1}}\right| .
\]
(If \( x_{1} y_{1} z_{1}=0 \), then the four planes do not bound a finite region.) Hence
\[
V^{2}=\frac{1}{36} a^{2} b^{2} c^{2}\left(\frac{x_{1}{ }^{2}}{a^{2}} \frac{y_{1}{ }^{2}}{b^{2}} \frac{z_{1}{ }^{2}}{c^{2}}\right)^{-1}
\]

But
\[
\left(\frac{x_{1}{ }^{2}}{a^{2}} \cdot \frac{y_{1}{ }^{2}}{b^{2}} \cdot \frac{z_{1}{ }^{2}}{c^{2}}\right)^{1 / 3} \leq \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{x_{1}{ }^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y_{1}{ }^{2}}{b^{2}}+\frac{z_{1}{ }^{2}}{c^{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{3}
\]
with equality if and only if
\[
\frac{x_{1}{ }^{2}}{a^{2}}=\frac{y_{1}{ }^{2}}{b^{2}}=\frac{z_{1}{ }^{2}}{c^{2}}=\frac{1}{3}
\]
(the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality). Hence
\[
V^{2} \geq \frac{27}{36} a^{2} b^{2} c^{2} \quad \text { and } \quad V \geq \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{3} a b c
\]
with equality if and only if \( \left(x_{1}, y_{1}, z_{1}\right) \) is one of the eight points for which (3) holds, namely
\[
( \pm a / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm b / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm c / \sqrt{3}) .
\]

It is also possible, of course, to minimize \( V \) straightforwardly by maximizing the product \( x_{1} y_{1} z_{1} \) under the constraint that \( \left(x_{1}, y_{1}, z_{1}\right) \) is a point

descriptive_long.question:
5. Find the smallest volume bounded by the coordinate planes and by a tangent plane to the ellipsoid
\[
\frac{\abscissa^{2}}{\semiaxisx^{2}}+\frac{\ordinate^{2}}{\semiaxisy^{2}}+\frac{\altitude^{2}}{\semiaxisz^{2}}=1
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The tangent plane to the given ellipsoid at the point \( \left(\tangentx, \tangenty, \tangentz\right) \) has the equation
\[
\frac{\abscissa \tangentx}{\semiaxisx^{2}}+\frac{\ordinate \tangenty}{\semiaxisy^{2}}+\frac{\altitude \tangentz}{\semiaxisz^{2}}=1 .
\]

Its intercepts on the \( \abscissa, \ordinate \), and \( \altitude \)-axes, respectively, are
\[
\frac{\semiaxisx^{2}}{\tangentx}, \frac{\semiaxisy^{2}}{\tangenty}, \frac{\semiaxisz^{2}}{\tangentz}
\]

The volume of the solid cut off by the tangent plane and the three coordinate planes is
\[
\volumevar=\frac{1}{6}\left|\frac{\semiaxisx^{2} \semiaxisy^{2} \semiaxisz^{2}}{\tangentx \tangenty \tangentz}\right| .
\]
(If \( \tangentx \tangenty \tangentz=0 \), then the four planes do not bound a finite region.) Hence
\[
\volumevar^{2}=\frac{1}{36} \semiaxisx^{2} \semiaxisy^{2} \semiaxisz^{2}\left(\frac{\tangentx^{2}}{\semiaxisx^{2}} \frac{\tangenty^{2}}{\semiaxisy^{2}} \frac{\tangentz^{2}}{\semiaxisz^{2}}\right)^{-1}
\]

But
\[
\left(\frac{\tangentx^{2}}{\semiaxisx^{2}} \cdot \frac{\tangenty^{2}}{\semiaxisy^{2}} \cdot \frac{\tangentz^{2}}{\semiaxisz^{2}}\right)^{1 / 3} \leq \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{\tangentx^{2}}{\semiaxisx^{2}}+\frac{\tangenty^{2}}{\semiaxisy^{2}}+\frac{\tangentz^{2}}{\semiaxisz^{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{3}
\]
with equality if and only if
\[
\frac{\tangentx^{2}}{\semiaxisx^{2}}=\frac{\tangenty^{2}}{\semiaxisy^{2}}=\frac{\tangentz^{2}}{\semiaxisz^{2}}=\frac{1}{3}
\]
(the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality). Hence
\[
\volumevar^{2} \geq \frac{27}{36} \semiaxisx^{2} \semiaxisy^{2} \semiaxisz^{2} \quad \text { and } \quad \volumevar \geq \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{3} \semiaxisx \semiaxisy \semiaxisz
\]
with equality if and only if \( \left(\tangentx, \tangenty, \tangentz\right) \) is one of the eight points for which (3) holds, namely
\[
( \pm \semiaxisx / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm \semiaxisy / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm \semiaxisz / \sqrt{3}) .
\]

It is also possible, of course, to minimize \( \volumevar \) straightforwardly by maximizing the product \( \tangentx \tangenty \tangentz \) under the constraint that \( \left(\tangentx, \tangenty, \tangentz\right) \) is a point

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Find the smallest volume bounded by the coordinate planes and by a tangent plane to the ellipsoid
\[
\frac{orangetree^{2}}{sandalwood^{2}}+\frac{blueberry^{2}}{elderberry^{2}}+\frac{watermelon^{2}}{gooseberry^{2}}=1
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The tangent plane to the given ellipsoid at the point \( \left(pineapple, strawberry, pomegranate\right) \) has the equation
\[
\frac{orangetree\,pineapple}{sandalwood^{2}}+\frac{blueberry\,strawberry}{elderberry^{2}}+\frac{watermelon\,pomegranate}{gooseberry^{2}}=1 .
\]

Its intercepts on the \( x, y \), and \( z \)-axes, respectively, are
\[
\frac{sandalwood^{2}}{pineapple}, \frac{elderberry^{2}}{strawberry}, \frac{gooseberry^{2}}{pomegranate}
\]

The volume of the solid cut off by the tangent plane and the three coordinate planes is
\[
raspberry=\frac{1}{6}\left|\frac{sandalwood^{2}\,elderberry^{2}\,gooseberry^{2}}{pineapple\,strawberry\,pomegranate}\right| .
\]
(If \( pineapple\,strawberry\,pomegranate=0 \), then the four planes do not bound a finite region.) Hence
\[
raspberry^{2}=\frac{1}{36}\,sandalwood^{2}\,elderberry^{2}\,gooseberry^{2}\left(\frac{pineapple{ }^{2}}{sandalwood^{2}} \frac{strawberry{ }^{2}}{elderberry^{2}} \frac{pomegranate{ }^{2}}{gooseberry^{2}}\right)^{-1}
\]

But
\[
\left(\frac{pineapple{ }^{2}}{sandalwood^{2}} \cdot \frac{strawberry{ }^{2}}{elderberry^{2}} \cdot \frac{pomegranate{ }^{2}}{gooseberry^{2}}\right)^{1 / 3} \leq \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{pineapple{ }^{2}}{sandalwood^{2}}+\frac{strawberry{ }^{2}}{elderberry^{2}}+\frac{pomegranate{ }^{2}}{gooseberry^{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{3}
\]
with equality if and only if
\[
\frac{pineapple{ }^{2}}{sandalwood^{2}}=\frac{strawberry{ }^{2}}{elderberry^{2}}=\frac{pomegranate{ }^{2}}{gooseberry^{2}}=\frac{1}{3}
\]
(the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality). Hence
\[
raspberry^{2} \geq \frac{27}{36} sandalwood^{2} elderberry^{2} gooseberry^{2} \quad \text { and } \quad raspberry \geq \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{3} sandalwood\,elderberry\,gooseberry
\]
with equality if and only if \( \left(pineapple, strawberry, pomegranate\right) \) is one of the eight points for which (3) holds, namely
\[
( \pm sandalwood / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm elderberry / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm gooseberry / \sqrt{3}) .
\]

It is also possible, of course, to minimize \( raspberry \) straightforwardly by maximizing the product \( pineapple\,strawberry\,pomegranate \) under the constraint that \( \left(pineapple, strawberry, pomegranate\right) \) is a point

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. Find the smallest volume bounded by the coordinate planes and by a tangent plane to the ellipsoid
\[
\frac{verticalaxis^{2}}{contractaxis^{2}}+\frac{longitudinalaxis^{2}}{narrowaxis^{2}}+\frac{planaraxis^{2}}{thinaxis^{2}}=1
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The tangent plane to the given ellipsoid at the point \( \left(verticalpoint, longitudinalpoint, planarpoint\right) \) has the equation
\[
\frac{verticalaxis\, verticalpoint}{contractaxis^{2}}+\frac{longitudinalaxis\, longitudinalpoint}{narrowaxis^{2}}+\frac{planaraxis\, planarpoint}{thinaxis^{2}}=1 .
\]

Its intercepts on the \( verticalaxis, longitudinalaxis \), and \( planaraxis \)-axes, respectively, are
\[
\frac{contractaxis^{2}}{verticalpoint}, \frac{narrowaxis^{2}}{longitudinalpoint}, \frac{thinaxis^{2}}{planarpoint}
\]

The volume of the solid cut off by the tangent plane and the three coordinate planes is
\[
voidvalue=\frac{1}{6}\left|\frac{contractaxis^{2} narrowaxis^{2} thinaxis^{2}}{verticalpoint longitudinalpoint planarpoint}\right| .
\]
(If \( verticalpoint longitudinalpoint planarpoint=0 \), then the four planes do not bound a finite region.) Hence
\[
voidvalue^{2}=\frac{1}{36} contractaxis^{2} narrowaxis^{2} thinaxis^{2}\left(\frac{verticalpoint{ }^{2}}{contractaxis^{2}} \frac{longitudinalpoint{ }^{2}}{narrowaxis^{2}} \frac{planarpoint{ }^{2}}{thinaxis^{2}}\right)^{-1}
\]

But
\[
\left(\frac{verticalpoint{ }^{2}}{contractaxis^{2}} \cdot \frac{longitudinalpoint{ }^{2}}{narrowaxis^{2}} \cdot \frac{planarpoint{ }^{2}}{thinaxis^{2}}\right)^{1 / 3} \leq \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{verticalpoint{ }^{2}}{contractaxis^{2}}+\frac{longitudinalpoint{ }^{2}}{narrowaxis^{2}}+\frac{planarpoint{ }^{2}}{thinaxis^{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{3}
\]
with equality if and only if
\[
\frac{verticalpoint{ }^{2}}{contractaxis^{2}}=\frac{longitudinalpoint{ }^{2}}{narrowaxis^{2}}=\frac{planarpoint{ }^{2}}{thinaxis^{2}}=\frac{1}{3}
\]
(the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality). Hence
\[
voidvalue^{2} \geq \frac{27}{36} contractaxis^{2} narrowaxis^{2} thinaxis^{2} \quad \text { and } \quad voidvalue \geq \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{3} contractaxis narrowaxis thinaxis
\]
with equality if and only if \( \left(verticalpoint, longitudinalpoint, planarpoint\right) \) is one of the eight points for which (3) holds, namely
\[
( \pm contractaxis / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm narrowaxis / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm thinaxis / \sqrt{3}) .
\]

It is also possible, of course, to minimize \( voidvalue \) straightforwardly by maximizing the product \( verticalpoint longitudinalpoint planarpoint \) under the constraint that \( \left(verticalpoint, longitudinalpoint, planarpoint\right) \) is a point

garbled_string.question:
5. Find the smallest volume bounded by the coordinate planes and by a tangent plane to the ellipsoid
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}}{pfwekrql^{2}}+\frac{hjgrksla^{2}}{zfqtbnma^{2}}+\frac{mndfqtly^{2}}{dxrglsvo^{2}}=1
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The tangent plane to the given ellipsoid at the point \( \left(vchplrwo, sgnkfduc, btqrwexy\right) \) has the equation
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnp vchplrwo}{pfwekrql^{2}}+\frac{hjgrksla sgnkfduc}{zfqtbnma^{2}}+\frac{mndfqtly btqrwexy}{dxrglsvo^{2}}=1 .
\]

Its intercepts on the \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla \), and \( mndfqtly \)-axes, respectively, are
\[
\frac{pfwekrql^{2}}{vchplrwo}, \frac{zfqtbnma^{2}}{sgnkfduc}, \frac{dxrglsvo^{2}}{btqrwexy}
\]

The volume of the solid cut off by the tangent plane and the three coordinate planes is
\[
ksjmztrp=\frac{1}{6}\left|\frac{pfwekrql^{2} zfqtbnma^{2} dxrglsvo^{2}}{vchplrwo sgnkfduc btqrwexy}\right| .
\]
(If \( vchplrwo sgnkfduc btqrwexy=0 \), then the four planes do not bound a finite region.) Hence
\[
ksjmztrp^{2}=\frac{1}{36} pfwekrql^{2} zfqtbnma^{2} dxrglsvo^{2}\left(\frac{vchplrwo^{2}}{pfwekrql^{2}} \frac{sgnkfduc^{2}}{zfqtbnma^{2}} \frac{btqrwexy^{2}}{dxrglsvo^{2}}\right)^{-1}
\]

But
\[
\left(\frac{vchplrwo^{2}}{pfwekrql^{2}} \cdot \frac{sgnkfduc^{2}}{zfqtbnma^{2}} \cdot \frac{btqrwexy^{2}}{dxrglsvo^{2}}\right)^{1 / 3} \leq \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{vchplrwo^{2}}{pfwekrql^{2}}+\frac{sgnkfduc^{2}}{zfqtbnma^{2}}+\frac{btqrwexy^{2}}{dxrglsvo^{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{3}
\]
with equality if and only if
\[
\frac{vchplrwo^{2}}{pfwekrql^{2}}=\frac{sgnkfduc^{2}}{zfqtbnma^{2}}=\frac{btqrwexy^{2}}{dxrglsvo^{2}}=\frac{1}{3}
\]
(the arithmetic-geometric mean inequality). Hence
\[
ksjmztrp^{2} \geq \frac{27}{36} pfwekrql^{2} zfqtbnma^{2} dxrglsvo^{2} \quad \text { and } \quad ksjmztrp \geq \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{3} pfwekrql zfqtbnma dxrglsvo
\]
with equality if and only if \( \left(vchplrwo, sgnkfduc, btqrwexy\right) \) is one of the eight points for which (3) holds, namely
\[
( \pm pfwekrql / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm zfqtbnma / \sqrt{3}, \quad \pm dxrglsvo / \sqrt{3}) .
\]

It is also possible, of course, to minimize \( ksjmztrp \) straightforwardly by maximizing the product \( vchplrwo sgnkfduc btqrwexy \) under the constraint that \( \left(vchplrwo, sgnkfduc, btqrwexy\right) \) is a point

kernel_variant.question:
Let $\lambda$ be a real number with $0<\lambda<1$ and consider in $\mathbb R^{6}$ the centred ``correlated'' ellipsoid  
\[
E_{\lambda}\;:\qquad \sum_{i=1}^{6}x_{i}^{2}+2\lambda\!\!\sum_{1\le i<j\le 6}\!x_{i}x_{j}=1. \tag{$\star$}
\]
Equivalently $E_{\lambda}$ is the quadric $x^{\mathsf T}Ax=1$, where
\[
A=(1-\lambda)I_{6}+\lambda J_{6},\qquad
0<\lambda<1 ,
\]
$I_{6}$ is the $6\times 6$ identity matrix and $J_{6}$ the $6\times 6$ all-ones matrix.

For a point $P=(p_{1},\dots ,p_{6})\in E_{\lambda}$ let $\Pi_{P}$ be the hyper-plane tangent to $E_{\lambda}$ at $P$.  
Together with the six coordinate hyper-planes $x_{1}=0,\dots ,x_{6}=0$, the plane $\Pi_{P}$ encloses a $6$-simplex $S(P)$ contained in the first orthant whenever that simplex is bounded.  
Denote its (six-dimensional) hyper-volume by $V(P)$.

(a)  Prove that $S(P)$ is bounded if and only if  
\[
(AP)_{i}>0\qquad\text{for all }i=1,\dots ,6 , \tag{1}
\]
i.e. iff every axis-intercept $t_{i}=1/(AP)_{i}$ of $\Pi_{P}$ is positive.  (No explicit sign condition on the $p_{i}$ is required.)

(b)  Determine, in closed form,
\[
V_{\min}= \min_{\substack{P\in E_{\lambda}\\ (AP)_{i}>0}} V(P), \tag{2}
\]
and describe precisely the set of all points $P$ at which that minimum is attained.  Express your answer solely in terms of $\lambda$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let $\boldsymbol 1=(1,\dots ,1)^{\mathsf T}\in\mathbb R^{6}$ and set $\Sigma:=\sum_{j=1}^{6}p_{j}$ for brevity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (a)  (boundedness criterion)

The tangent hyper-plane at $P\in E_{\lambda}$ is obtained from the gradient of the quadratic form $F(x)=x^{\mathsf T}Ax$:
\[
\nabla F(P)=2AP\quad\Longrightarrow\quad (AP)\cdot(x-P)=0.
\]
Because $P^{\mathsf T}AP=1$, this rewrites as
\[
(AP)\cdot x=1. \tag{3}
\]

Intercept with the $x_{i}$-axis: insert $x=t\,e_{i}$ in (3) to get $t_{i}=1/(AP)_{i}$.  
If some $(AP)_{i}\le 0$, then either $t_{i}\le 0$ or $t_{i}=\infty$; in both cases $\Pi_{P}$ fails to intersect the positive $x_{i}$-axis at a positive finite point, whence the coordinate hyper-planes cannot cut off a bounded region in the first orthant.  
Conversely, if every $(AP)_{i}>0$ then each $t_{i}$ is positive and finite, and the six coordinate hyper-planes together with $\Pi_{P}$ bound the right $6$-simplex
\[
S(P)=\bigl\{(x_{1},\dots ,x_{6})\;|\;x_{i}\ge 0,\;\; (AP)\cdot x\le 1\bigr\}
\]
within the first orthant.  Hence (1) is both necessary and sufficient for $S(P)$ to be bounded. $\square$

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b)  (minimisation of the volume)

Step 1.  Volume in terms of $AP$  
Because $S(P)$ is a right $6$-simplex whose edges along the positive coordinate axes have lengths $t_{i}=1/(AP)_{i}$, its hyper-volume is
\[
V(P)=\frac{1}{6!}\prod_{i=1}^{6}\frac{1}{(AP)_{i}}=\frac{1}{720}\prod_{i=1}^{6}(AP)_{i}^{\,-1}. \tag{4}
\]
Thus minimising $V(P)$ is equivalent to maximising
\[
\Phi(P):=\prod_{i=1}^{6}(AP)_{i} \tag{5}
\]
under the constraints $P\in E_{\lambda}$ and $(AP)_{i}>0$.

Step 2.  A convenient expression for $(AP)_{i}$  
Since $A$ has $1$ on the diagonal and $\lambda$ off the diagonal,
\[
(AP)_{i}=p_{i}+\lambda\!\!\sum_{j\ne i}\!p_{j}=(1-\lambda)p_{i}+\lambda\Sigma. \tag{6}
\]

Step 3.  Parameterisation by mean and deviation  
Write $P=t\boldsymbol 1+u$, where $u=(u_{1},\dots ,u_{6})$ satisfies $\boldsymbol 1^{\mathsf T}u=0$.  
Then $\Sigma=6t$ and (6) becomes
\[
(AP)_{i}=t(1+5\lambda)+(1-\lambda)u_{i}. \tag{7}
\]
Set
\[
\alpha:=t(1+5\lambda),\qquad \beta:=1-\lambda>0 .
\]
Hence
\[
(AP)_{i}= \alpha+\beta u_{i}. \tag{8}
\]

Step 4.  The ellipsoid constraint in the new variables  
Because $A=(1-\lambda)I_{6}+\lambda J_{6}$ is positive definite,
\[
1=P^{\mathsf T}AP=(t\boldsymbol 1+u)^{\mathsf T}A(t\boldsymbol 1+u)
        =6(1+5\lambda)t^{2}+\beta\,\lVert u\rVert^{2}. \tag{9}
\]
Solving (9) for $t^{2}$ gives
\[
t^{2}=\frac{1-\beta\lVert u\rVert^{2}}{6(1+5\lambda)}\quad\text{with}\quad
0\le\beta\lVert u\rVert^{2}\le 1. \tag{10}
\]
Consequently
\[
\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{1+5\lambda}{6}}\,
       \sqrt{1-\beta\lVert u\rVert^{2}}, \quad 0\le\beta\lVert u\rVert^{2}\le 1. \tag{11}
\]
Observe that $\alpha$ is a strictly decreasing function of $\lVert u\rVert^{2}$.

Step 5.  Maximising $\Phi$  

We have
\[
\Phi(P)=\prod_{i=1}^{6}\bigl(\alpha+\beta u_{i}\bigr). \tag{12}
\]
Because $\sum_{i=1}^{6}u_{i}=0$, the numbers $\alpha+\beta u_{i}$ share the arithmetic mean $\alpha$.  The AM-GM inequality yields
\[
\Phi(P)\le \alpha^{6}; \tag{13}
\]
equality in (13) occurs if and only if every $u_{i}=0$.  
But by (11) $\alpha$ itself is maximised when $\lVert u\rVert^{2}=0$.  
Combining both facts shows
\[
\Phi(P)\le \alpha^{6}\le \alpha_{0}^{6},\qquad
\alpha_{0}:=\sqrt{\frac{1+5\lambda}{6}}, \tag{14}
\]
with equality throughout if and only if $u=0$.  Hence every maximiser of $\Phi$ (and therefore every minimiser of $V$) must satisfy
\[
P=t\boldsymbol 1\quad\text{with}\quad u=0. \tag{15}
\]

Step 6.  Determining $t$  
Insert $P=t\boldsymbol 1$ into the constraint $P^{\mathsf T}AP=1$:
\[
1=6t^{2}+2\lambda\cdot 15t^{2}=6(1+5\lambda)t^{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
t=\pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{6(1+5\lambda)}}. \tag{16}
\]
Step (a) demands $(AP)_{i}>0$; by (7) this reads $(1+5\lambda)t>0$, hence $t>0$.  Therefore the unique feasible optimiser is
\[
P_{*}=\frac{\boldsymbol 1}{\sqrt{6(1+5\lambda)}}. \tag{17}
\]

Step 7.  Minimal volume  
Using (4) at $P_{*}$ and $(AP_{*})_{i}=\alpha_{0}$,
\[
V_{\min}= \frac{1}{720}\,\alpha_{0}^{-6}
        =\frac{1}{720}\left(\frac{6}{1+5\lambda}\right)^{3}
        =\frac{216}{720}(1+5\lambda)^{-3}
        =\frac{3}{10}\,(1+5\lambda)^{-3}. \tag{18}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Result  

\[
\boxed{\,V_{\min}=\dfrac{3}{10}\,(1+5\lambda)^{-3}\,},\qquad
\boxed{\,P_{*}=\dfrac{(1,\dots ,1)}{\sqrt{6(1+5\lambda)}}\,}.
\]
The volume $V(P)$ attains its minimum if and only if $P=P_{*}$; for all other feasible $P$ one has $V(P)>V_{\min}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with both the original three–dimensional problem and the current four–dimensional kernel variant, this enhancement is markedly tougher for several reasons.

• Higher dimension: the problem is lifted to six dimensions, inflating the combinatorial and algebraic workload (e.g. 6! = 720 factors into the volume formula).

• Non-diagonal quadric: the ellipsoid contains equal cross terms controlled by λ, so the gradient and tangent-plane intercepts are no longer simple coordinate scalings.  One must manipulate the full matrix A with off-diagonal entries, which forces the introduction of linear‐form products (A P)_i rather than single coordinates.

• Symmetry yet non-orthogonality: while A is permutation-invariant, its non-orthogonal nature obliges a careful symmetry argument (or an appeal to convex optimisation theory) to justify that the extremum occurs at equal coordinates.  A naive arithmetic–geometric mean inequality does not suffice.

• Advanced optimisation: maximising the product of six affine forms under a quadratic constraint demands either Lagrange multipliers in matrix language or a convexity/majorisation argument, both substantially more sophisticated than the scalar AM–GM step used in the original.

• Final expression: obtaining an explicit closed-form minimum volume, (3/10)(1+5λ)^{−3}, requires tracking the dependence on λ through each stage, including the non-trivial evaluation of the quadratic constraint and the intercepts.

These layers of additional structure and proof complexity raise the problem well beyond “plug-and-play” techniques, satisfying all the stated guidelines for a significantly more difficult kernel variant.

Conversely, if every $(AP)_{i}>0$ then each $t_{i}$ is positive and finite, and the six coordinate hyper-planes together with $\Pi_{P}$ bound the right $6$-simplex
\[
S(P)=\bigl\{(x_{1},\dots ,x_{6})\;|\;x_{i}\ge 0,\;\; (AP)\cdot x\le 1\bigr\}
\]
within the first orthant.  Hence (1) is both necessary and sufficient for $S(P)$ to be bounded. $\square$

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b)  (minimisation of the volume)

Step 1.  Volume in terms of $AP$  
Because $S(P)$ is a right $6$-simplex whose edges along the positive coordinate axes have lengths $t_{i}=1/(AP)_{i}$, its hyper-volume is
\[
V(P)=\frac{1}{6!}\prod_{i=1}^{6}\frac{1}{(AP)_{i}}=\frac{1}{720}\prod_{i=1}^{6}(AP)_{i}^{\,-1}. \tag{4}
\]
Thus minimising $V(P)$ is equivalent to maximising
\[
\Phi(P):=\prod_{i=1}^{6}(AP)_{i} \tag{5}
\]
under the constraints $P\in E_{\lambda}$ and $(AP)_{i}>0$.

Step 2.  A convenient expression for $(AP)_{i}$  
Since $A$ has $1$ on the diagonal and $\lambda$ off the diagonal,
\[
(AP)_{i}=p_{i}+\lambda\!\!\sum_{j\ne i}\!p_{j}=(1-\lambda)p_{i}+\lambda\Sigma. \tag{6}
\]

Step 3.  Parameterisation by mean and deviation  
Write $P=t\boldsymbol 1+u$, where $u=(u_{1},\dots ,u_{6})$ satisfies $\boldsymbol 1^{\mathsf T}u=0$.  
Then $\Sigma=6t$ and (6) becomes
\[
(AP)_{i}=t(1+5\lambda)+(1-\lambda)u_{i}. \tag{7}
\]
Set
\[
\alpha:=t(1+5\lambda),\qquad \beta:=1-\lambda>0 .
\]
Hence
\[
(AP)_{i}= \alpha+\beta u_{i}. \tag{8}
\]

Step 4.  The ellipsoid constraint in the new variables  
Because $A=(1-\lambda)I_{6}+\lambda J_{6}$ is positive definite,
\[
1=P^{\mathsf T}AP=(t\boldsymbol 1+u)^{\mathsf T}A(t\boldsymbol 1+u)
        =6(1+5\lambda)t^{2}+\beta\,\lVert u\rVert^{2}. \tag{9}
\]
Solving (9) for $t^{2}$ gives
\[
t^{2}=\frac{1-\beta\lVert u\rVert^{2}}{6(1+5\lambda)}\quad\text{with}\quad
0\le\beta\lVert u\rVert^{2}\le 1. \tag{10}
\]
Consequently
\[
\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{1+5\lambda}{6}}\,
       \sqrt{1-\beta\lVert u\rVert^{2}}, \quad 0\le\beta\lVert u\rVert^{2}\le 1. \tag{11}
\]
Observe that $\alpha$ is a strictly decreasing function of $\lVert u\rVert^{2}$.

Step 5.  Maximising $\Phi$  

We have
\[
\Phi(P)=\prod_{i=1}^{6}\bigl(\alpha+\beta u_{i}\bigr). \tag{12}
\]
Because $\sum_{i=1}^{6}u_{i}=0$, the numbers $\alpha+\beta u_{i}$ share the arithmetic mean $\alpha$.  The AM-GM inequality yields
\[
\Phi(P)\le \alpha^{6}; \tag{13}
\]
equality in (13) occurs if and only if every $u_{i}=0$.  
But by (11) $\alpha$ itself is maximised when $\lVert u\rVert^{2}=0$.  
Combining both facts shows
\[
\Phi(P)\le \alpha^{6}\le \alpha_{0}^{6},\qquad
\alpha_{0}:=\sqrt{\frac{1+5\lambda}{6}}, \tag{14}
\]
with equality throughout if and only if $u=0$.  Hence every maximiser of $\Phi$ (and therefore every minimiser of $V$) must satisfy
\[
P=t\boldsymbol 1\quad\text{with}\quad u=0. \tag{15}
\]

Step 6.  Determining $t$  
Insert $P=t\boldsymbol 1$ into the constraint $P^{\mathsf T}AP=1$:
\[
1=6t^{2}+2\lambda\cdot 15t^{2}=6(1+5\lambda)t^{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
t=\pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{6(1+5\lambda)}}. \tag{16}
\]
Step (a) demands $(AP)_{i}>0$; by (7) this reads $(1+5\lambda)t>0$, hence $t>0$.  Therefore the unique feasible optimiser is
\[
P_{*}=\frac{\boldsymbol 1}{\sqrt{6(1+5\lambda)}}. \tag{17}
\]

Step 7.  Minimal volume  
Using (4) at $P_{*}$ and $(AP_{*})_{i}=\alpha_{0}$,
\[
V_{\min}= \frac{1}{720}\,\alpha_{0}^{-6}
        =\frac{1}{720}\left(\frac{6}{1+5\lambda}\right)^{3}
        =\frac{216}{720}(1+5\lambda)^{-3}
        =\frac{3}{10}\,(1+5\lambda)^{-3}. \tag{18}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Result  

\[
\boxed{\,V_{\min}=\dfrac{3}{10}\,(1+5\lambda)^{-3}\,},\qquad
\boxed{\,P_{*}=\dfrac{(1,\dots ,1)}{\sqrt{6(1+5\lambda)}}\,}.
\]
The volume $V(P)$ attains its minimum if and only if $P=P_{*}$; for all other feasible $P$ one has $V(P)>V_{\min}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with both the original three–dimensional problem and the current four–dimensional kernel variant, this enhancement is markedly tougher for several reasons.

• Higher dimension: the problem is lifted to six dimensions, inflating the combinatorial and algebraic workload (e.g. 6! = 720 factors into the volume formula).

• Non-diagonal quadric: the ellipsoid contains equal cross terms controlled by λ, so the gradient and tangent-plane intercepts are no longer simple coordinate scalings.  One must manipulate the full matrix A with off-diagonal entries, which forces the introduction of linear‐form products (A P)_i rather than single coordinates.

• Symmetry yet non-orthogonality: while A is permutation-invariant, its non-orthogonal nature obliges a careful symmetry argument (or an appeal to convex optimisation theory) to justify that the extremum occurs at equal coordinates.  A naive arithmetic–geometric mean inequality does not suffice.

• Advanced optimisation: maximising the product of six affine forms under a quadratic constraint demands either Lagrange multipliers in matrix language or a convexity/majorisation argument, both substantially more sophisticated than the scalar AM–GM step used in the original.

• Final expression: obtaining an explicit closed-form minimum volume, (3/10)(1+5λ)^{−3}, requires tracking the dependence on λ through each stage, including the non-trivial evaluation of the quadratic constraint and the intercepts.

These layers of additional structure and proof complexity raise the problem well beyond “plug-and-play” techniques, satisfying all the stated guidelines for a significantly more difficult kernel variant.
